title: SBOM attestation completeness — critical vendors without signed SBOM
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate the content of the SBOM (covered by 22.43.5); focuses on presence/signature.
evidence: Saved search 'sbom_attestation_completeness' (weekly) lists critical vendors without a valid signed SBOM.

compliance.assurance_rationale: EU CRA mandates SBOM provisioning and signature.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that EU-CRA Art.13 (Obligations of manufacturers) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.5: SBOM attestation completeness — critical vendors without signed SBOM.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.44.5' running on Vendor SBOM register (vendor_sbom.csv), signature-validation tooling outputs, VRM criticality., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIS2 supply-chain risk management is directly supported.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.5: SBOM attestation completeness — critical vendors without signed SBOM.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.44.5' running on Vendor SBOM register (vendor_sbom.csv), signature-validation tooling outputs, VRM criticality., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST-800-53 SR-11(1) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.5: SBOM attestation completeness — critical vendors without signed SBOM.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.44.5' running on Vendor SBOM register (vendor_sbom.csv), signature-validation tooling outputs, VRM criticality., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A critical vendor has no SBOM in the register; the UC fires weekly until resolved.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A critical vendor has a signed SBOM within policy cadence; no alert.

dataSources: Vendor SBOM register (vendor_sbom.csv), signature-validation tooling outputs, VRM criticality.
spl:
| inputlookup vrm_attestations.csv
| where criticality="critical"
| lookup vendor_sbom_attestations.csv vendor OUTPUT sbom_sha256 signed signed_by signed_at
| eval age_days=round((now()-strptime(signed_at,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400,0)
| where isnull(sbom_sha256) OR signed!="true" OR age_days>180
| table vendor criticality sbom_sha256 signed signed_by age_days
description: Surfaces critical vendors whose SBOM is absent, unsigned, or older than policy cadence (default 180 days).
value: Makes the CRA/NIS2 SBOM requirement a tracked operational metric.
implementation: (1) Onboard vendor SBOMs through procurement workflow; (2) validate signatures with sigstore or equivalent; (3) run weekly. Lookups: `vendor_sbom_attestations`, `vendor_sbom_attestations.csv`, `vrm_attestations`, `vrm_attestations.csv`.
visualization: Table of at-risk vendors, bar chart of signature freshness, single-value 'critical vendors with signed SBOM'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `vendor_sbom_attestations.csv`, `vrm_attestations.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('vendor_sbom_attestations.csv','vrm_attestations.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.13`. The control objective is: "Evidence that EU-CRA Art.13 (Obligations of manufacturers) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.5: SBOM attestation completeness — critical vendors without signed SBOM." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Onboard vendor SBOMs through procurement workflow. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 2:** validate signatures with sigstore or equivalent. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** run weekly. Lookups: `vendor_sbom_attestations`, `vendor_sbom_attestations.csv`, `vrm_attestations`, `vrm_attestations.csv`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "SBOM attestation completeness — critical vendors without signed SBOM," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of EU-CRA control activity.

This search begins with `| inputlookup vrm_attestations.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `vrm_attestations.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup vrm_attestations.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true vrm_attestations_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the full search range) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.44.5: SBOM attestation completeness — critical vendors without sig]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.44.5,reg=EU-CRA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `vendor`, `sbom_sha256`, `signed`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table vendor, sbom_sha256, signed
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `vendor_sbom_attestations.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup vendor_sbom_attestations.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `age_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(age_days) as p50 perc90(age_days) as p90 perc99(age_days) as p99 max(age_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `180` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.44.5: EU-CRA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of At-Risk Vendors (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Signature Freshness (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Critical Vendors With Signed Sbom' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**EU-CRA-specific failure modes:**

• Product scope: Cyber Resilience Act applies to 'products with digital elements.' The scope depends on whether the product is placed on the EU market — a product sold only within an enterprise isn't in scope. Reference `cra_product_scope.csv`.

• Important vs Critical product designation: CRA has three tiers (default / important / critical). Detections calibrated for critical products over-apply to default; separate thresholds by designation.

• Conformity assessment route: CRA offers internal control or third-party assessment routes. The evidence the detection produces must match the chosen route; internal-control route requires more detailed internal evidence than the third-party route.

• Vulnerability disclosure (Art. 11): manufacturer obligation to actively exploit-monitor. Detections on exploitation attempts must feed the vulnerability disclosure workflow; a detection without a pipeline to the disclosure process is insufficient.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when third-party risk scores move so you can act before a weak vendor becomes a real problem.
